name: Refined Carbohydrates
description: White bread, rice, pasta, and pastries rapidly digest into glucose. Without fiber or protein to slow absorption, they cause steep blood sugar spikes within 30–45 minutes.
avoidanceTips: Replace with whole-grain versions (GI reduction of 20–30 points)
Combine with protein or fat to slow digestion
Reduce portion size by 30–50%
Choose pasta al dente — less broken down, lower GI

name: Sugary Beverages
description: Liquid sugars — juices, sodas, energy drinks, and sweetened coffees — bypass the normal digestion delay. They spike glucose faster and higher than solid foods with equivalent carbs.
avoidanceTips: Replace juice with whole fruit — fiber slows absorption
Choose sparkling water with lemon instead of soda
Use unsweetened coffee/tea with a splash of cream
If you must drink juice, limit to 4 oz and pair with protein

name: Oversized Meal Portions
description: Even healthy foods spike glucose when eaten in large quantities. The total glycemic load of a meal matters more than any single ingredient.
avoidanceTips: Use the plate method: ½ vegetables, ¼ protein, ¼ carbs
Eat slowly — satiety signals take 20 minutes
Start with a salad or soup to reduce main-course intake
Use smaller plates to reduce portion sizes naturally

name: Sitting After Meals
description: Muscle contractions during movement help cells absorb glucose independently of insulin. Sitting still after a meal allows glucose to accumulate in the bloodstream.
examples: Eating at a desk then working
avoidanceTips: Walk 10–15 minutes after each meal
Do light activity like standing or slow pacing
Schedule calls as walking meetings after lunch
Even 100 squats post-meal significantly improves TIR

name: Stress & High Cortisol
description: Cortisol — the stress hormone — triggers gluconeogenesis (liver glucose release) and reduces insulin sensitivity. Emotional or work stress can raise fasting glucose by 20–40 mg/dL.
avoidanceTips: Practice 4-7-8 breathing: inhale 4s, hold 7s, exhale 8s
Schedule regular decompression breaks
Physical exercise is the fastest cortisol flush
Monitor glucose during known stressful periods

name: Poor or Short Sleep
description: Even one night of poor sleep reduces insulin sensitivity by up to 25%. Growth hormone secretion during deep sleep is critical for glucose regulation. Chronic sleep debt raises A1C.
avoidanceTips: Target 7–9 hours of consistent sleep nightly
Keep a fixed wake time, even on weekends
Avoid screens 60 min before bed
Cool bedroom (65–68°F) improves deep sleep quality

name: Eating Too Quickly
description: Faster eating correlates with larger spikes independent of food type. Thorough chewing begins starch digestion in the mouth and signals incretin hormones that blunt glucose rise.
avoidanceTips: Target 20+ minutes for each meal
Put down utensils between bites
Chew each bite 20–30 times
No phones or screens during meals

name: Dawn Phenomenon
description: Between 4–8 AM, the body releases growth hormone and cortisol to prepare for waking. This triggers hepatic glucose release, raising fasting glucose by 15–30 mg/dL even without eating.
examples: Elevated fasting glucose despite good evening levels
Morning readings higher than midnight levels
avoidanceTips: Light evening snack with protein can blunt it
Morning exercise quickly uses the released glucose
Avoid high-carb breakfasts that compound the effect
Consistent sleep/wake timing helps stabilize the pattern

name: Alcohol & Rebound Effect
description: Alcohol initially lowers glucose (liver prioritizes alcohol metabolism over gluconeogenesis). The rebound spike 3–8 hours later can be significant, especially with sugary mixers.
avoidanceTips: Choose dry wine or spirits with sugar-free mixers
Never drink on an empty stomach
Monitor glucose 4–6 hours after drinking
Limit to 1–2 standard drinks

name: Illness & Infection
description: The immune response to illness releases counter-regulatory hormones (cortisol, glucagon, epinephrine) that raise blood glucose, sometimes significantly, even without eating.
avoidanceTips: Increase glucose monitoring frequency when sick
Stay well-hydrated
Contact your healthcare provider for sick day rules
Prioritize sleep and rest for faster recovery
